description:
You are acting as a “configuration-manager” who must track – and prove – the exact
    changes made to a local JSON configuration file by exercising it through curl
    “API–style” requests that use the file:// URI-scheme.  
    (curl understands file://, so this exercise stays completely off-line yet still
    looks exactly like normal API work.)

    Goal
    ----
    1. Create a brand-new workspace directory
          /home/user/config_manager
       containing all artefacts that follow.
    2. Build an initial configuration file called
          /home/user/config_manager/config.json
       whose precise byte-for-byte contents must be **exactly**

          {"version":1,"service":"auth","enabled":true}

       • No spaces, no line-breaks, no trailing newline.
    3. “Retrieve” that configuration through curl and store the received body in a
       file named
          /home/user/config_manager/responses/initial_config.json
       (you will have to mkdir the responses/ sub-directory yourself).
    4. “Patch” the configuration by changing only the value of the version key from
       1 → 2.  The file that ultimately lives at the original path
          /home/user/config_manager/config.json
       must therefore end up containing

          {"version":2,"service":"auth","enabled":true}

       (again, no spaces or line breaks).
    5. Make a second curl request (GET is fine) to
          file:///home/user/config_manager/config.json
       and store that body in
          /home/user/config_manager/responses/final_config.json
    6. Build an audit log called
          /home/user/config_manager/audit.log
       in **exactly** the following pipe-delimited format – *one line per curl action
       in the exact chronological order they were executed*:

          <ISO-8601-UTC-timestamp>|<ACTION>|<URI>|<HTTP-status>|<MD5-of-body>

       where
         • ISO-8601-UTC-timestamp is to second resolution, e.g.
           2024-04-03T15:30:27Z
         • ACTION must be either GET or PATCH to reflect what you did.
         • URI must literally be
           file:///home/user/config_manager/config.json
         • HTTP-status is what curl reports using %{http_code}.  For a file://
           request this is always 000 (three zeros).
         • MD5-of-body is the 32-character lowercase hexadecimal MD5 checksum of
           the *response body* that you actually wrote to the corresponding
           responses/*.json file.
       Therefore audit.log will contain **exactly two lines** – one for the initial
       GET, one for the “PATCH” (the second curl GET that verifies the change).

    7. All three generated files must be world-readable:
          -rw-r--r-- 1 user user …

    Verification the automatic test will perform
    --------------------------------------------
    •   Existence of directory  /home/user/config_manager
    •   Existence and byte-for-byte contents of
            /home/user/config_manager/config.json        (final version == 2)
            /home/user/config_manager/responses/initial_config.json
            /home/user/config_manager/responses/final_config.json
    •   audit.log exists and:
            – has exactly two lines,
            – is pipe-delimited into five fields,
            – fields 2,3,4 are respectively  GET/PATCH   file:///…   000,
            – the MD5 values match the actual MD5 of the corresponding
              responses/*.json bodies.
    •   The MD5 recorded for line-1 must match the MD5 of
            responses/initial_config.json   (=version 1),
        and line-2 must match the MD5 of
            responses/final_config.json      (=version 2).
    •   permissions of all new files are 644.

    The agent may use any tools available in a typical Linux environment (echo,
    jq, sed, md5sum, date, etc.) but **must** rely on curl for the “API” steps, and
    must NOT invoke sudo or require any network connectivity.
truth:
Final required file layout and contents (after the agent finishes):

    1. /home/user/config_manager/
           ├── config.json
           ├── audit.log
           └── responses/
                ├── initial_config.json
                └── final_config.json

    2. File contents
       2.1 /home/user/config_manager/config.json
           {"version":2,"service":"auth","enabled":true}

       2.2 /home/user/config_manager/responses/initial_config.json
           {"version":1,"service":"auth","enabled":true}

       2.3 /home/user/config_manager/responses/final_config.json
           {"version":2,"service":"auth","enabled":true}

    3. audit.log must have exactly two lines, each of the form
           2024-04-03T15:30:27Z|GET|file:///home/user/config_manager/config.json|000|<32-hex-MD5>
           2024-04-03T15:30:31Z|PATCH|file:///home/user/config_manager/config.json|000|<32-hex-MD5>
       The two MD5 values, when recomputed over the respective response
       body files, must match those found in the log.

    4. Permissions for all newly-created files are 0644 (-rw-r--r--).

    NOTE:  the automated test framework will recompute the MD5 checksums directly
    from the responses/*.json files and compare them with the values recorded in
    audit.log, so the literal checksum strings do not need to be hard-coded here.
name: task_000000_4dca16e6
